name: Package Python Apps in Docker with Multi-Stage Builds
description: Containerize Python applications using Docker multi-stage builds for small, secure, production-ready images.
prerequisites: A Python project with requirements.txt or pyproject.toml

steps.title: Create a Dockerfile with multi-stage build
steps.command:
cat > Dockerfile << 'EOF'
# Build stage
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

# Production stage
FROM python:3.12-slim
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
COPY src/ ./src/
EXPOSE 8000
CMD ["python", "-m", "src.main"]
EOF
steps.description: Create a multi-stage Dockerfile. The builder stage installs dependencies, and the production stage copies only the installed packages, keeping the final image small.
steps.tip: Using python:3.12-slim instead of python:3.12 reduces the base image size from ~1GB to ~150MB.

steps.title: Create a .dockerignore file
steps.description: Exclude unnecessary files from the Docker build context. This speeds up builds and prevents sensitive files from being included in the image.

steps.title: Build the Docker image
steps.description: Build the Docker image from the Dockerfile in the current directory. Docker caches each layer, so subsequent builds with unchanged requirements are fast.

steps.title: Run the container
steps.description: Start a container from the image in detached mode, mapping port 8000 on the host to port 8000 in the container.

steps.title: Check the image size
steps.description: Verify the final image size. A well-optimized Python image with slim base should be under 200MB for most applications.
steps.tip: If you need an even smaller image, consider python:3.12-alpine, but be aware that some Python packages with C extensions may require additional build dependencies.

fullScript:
#!/bin/bash
# Docker packaging workflow for Python apps
set -e

# Create Dockerfile
cat > Dockerfile << 'DOCKERFILE'
# Build stage - install dependencies
FROM python:3.12-slim AS builder
WORKDIR /build
COPY requirements.txt .
RUN pip install --no-cache-dir --prefix=/install -r requirements.txt

# Production stage - copy only what's needed
FROM python:3.12-slim
RUN useradd --create-home appuser
WORKDIR /app

COPY --from=builder /install /usr/local
COPY src/ ./src/

USER appuser
EXPOSE 8000
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s CMD python -c "import urllib.request; urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8000/health')"
CMD ["python", "-m", "src.main"]
DOCKERFILE

# Create .dockerignore
cat > .dockerignore << 'IGNORE'
.venv
__pycache__
*.pyc
.git
.pytest_cache
.mypy_cache
.ruff_cache
*.egg-info
dist
build
IGNORE

docker build -t myapp:latest .
docker images myapp

echo "Docker image built successfully."

faq.question: Why use multi-stage builds for Python?
faq.answer: Multi-stage builds let you install build tools and compile dependencies in a temporary stage, then copy only the installed packages to a clean final image. This reduces image size and removes build-time tools that could be security risks.

faq.question: Should I use Alpine or Slim as the base image?
faq.answer: Use python:slim (Debian-based) as the default. Alpine uses musl instead of glibc, which can cause compatibility issues with compiled Python packages like numpy, pandas, and cryptography. Alpine images are smaller but often require more build dependencies.

faq.question: How do I handle Python packages that need system libraries?
faq.answer: Install system dependencies with apt-get in the builder stage. For the production stage, copy only the runtime libraries (not build tools). For example: RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends libpq5 for PostgreSQL support.
